The OSCOLA
Chronicles
A Legal Citation Adventure

Five chapters of narrative learning, interactive exercises and a ten-question bar examination.

© Susan Nellany-Gibbs 2026
Chapter I · Page 1 of 3 · The Brief Arrives

It is 7.42 am at
Gray's Inn.

Rain taps against the tall sash windows. The gas lamps are still lit. You are Eleanor Marsh, a first-six pupil, and today is your first day in chambers. You arrived early, hoping to impress. The clerk's room smells of beeswax and old leather, and somewhere upstairs a printer is grinding through what sounds like a very long skeleton argument.

A thick envelope lands on your desk with a thud. Inside: forty pages of draft submissions, each one studded with references that look almost right. Almost. You scan the first footnote and feel a creeping unease.

Senior Clerk, Mr Devereaux Miss Marsh. Your supervisor, Ms Okafor QC, expects those citations corrected by ten o'clock. Every last one. She is particular about OSCOLA. The judges notice. So does she.

You have heard of OSCOLA in your LLB. The Oxford Standard for the Citation of Legal Authorities. A single unified system used across UK law schools, barristers' chambers, solicitors' firms, and the appellate courts. Precise, unforgiving, elegant. You know the name. Now you must know the rules.

Chapter I · Page 2 of 3 · First Principles

Ms Okafor arrives.
She does not knock.

Ms Okafor QC · Chapter I

She sets a slim booklet on your desk: the OSCOLA Quick Reference Guide. Fourth edition. She taps the cover once.

Ms Okafor QC Before you touch a single citation, learn the three rules that govern everything else. Do not use full stops in abbreviations. Separate multiple citations with a semicolon. And when you pinpoint, give paragraph numbers in square brackets at the end.

She is already walking away. You open the booklet. Three rules, clear as crystal:

.
No Full Stops
Write AC not A.C. Write QB not Q.B. Write s not s. This applies to every abbreviation throughout OSCOLA.
;
Semicolons Between Citations
When listing multiple authorities in a single footnote, separate them with a semicolon: Case A; Case B; Statute C
[42]
Pinpoint Paragraphs
Give paragraph numbers in square brackets at the end of a citation. If there are no paragraph numbers, give the page pinpoint instead.

You write these three rules at the top of a fresh page of your notebook. They will govern everything that follows.

Chapter I · Page 3 of 3 · The Hierarchy of Reports

Which law report
do you cite?

The skeleton argument cites several cases using reports that are available, but not the preferred ones. Ms Okafor has underlined each one in red. There is a hierarchy, she has scrawled in the margin, and you must follow it.

Citation Hierarchy 1. Neutral citation (if available), then the
2. Official Law Reports: AC, Ch, QB, Fam, UKSC
3. If not in Law Reports: All ER or WLR
4. If not above: a specialist report
Never cite an unreported case if a report exists

Neutral citations were introduced in 2001. They look like this: [2008] UKHL 13. They are assigned by the court itself and allow the case to be found independent of any publisher's series. When a case has a neutral citation, you give it first, followed by the preferred law report citation.

Correct Order: Neutral + Law Report Corr v IBC Vehicles Ltd [2008] UKHL 13, [2008] 1 AC 884
Neutral citation comes first, law report second

Page v Smith [1996] AC 155 (HL)
No neutral citation (pre-2001): give court in brackets after

You look down at the draft. Three of the footnotes cite only the WLR when the Law Reports citation exists. You reach for your red pen.

Chapter II · Page 1 of 3 · Anatomy of a Case Citation

Ms Okafor writes a name
on the whiteboard.

Generating scene...
AI-generated · Gavel and scales of justice, candlelit

She writes: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562. Then she turns, chalk in hand.

Ms Okafor QC Every case has bones. Party names. Year. Report series. First page. Once you know the bones, you can cite any case. Hover over each part. Learn what it means.

She gestures to the whiteboard. You step closer. Hover over each element below to reveal its rule.

HOVER EACH PART TO REVEAL ITS RULE

Corr v IBC Vehicles LtdParty names: not italicised in OSCOLA. Use ‘v’ not ‘vs’ or ‘v.’ [2008]Square brackets [ ] = year needed to identify the volume of the report UKHL 13Neutral citation: court code + case number. Assigned by the court itself (post-2001). , [2008]Year of the law report volume (in square brackets again) 1Volume number within the year (some series have more than one volume per year) ACReport series: Appeal Cases. The most authoritative series in the Law Reports. 884First page of the case in the report. Not a pinpoint; the case starts here.
Chapter II · Page 2 of 3 · Pinpointing

You need a specific paragraph.
How do you point to it?

The skeleton refers to particular passages in two cases. Ms Okafor leans over your shoulder.

Ms Okafor QC A pinpoint tells the reader exactly where the proposition you rely on appears. If the judgment has paragraph numbers, use those in square brackets at the end. If there are no paragraph numbers, give the page after the court.
Pinpointing with Paragraph Numbers Callery v Gray [2001] EWCA Civ 1117, [2001] 1 WLR 2112 [42], [45]
Two specific paragraphs, each in square brackets

Bunt v Tilley [2006] EWHC 407 (QB), [2006] 3 All ER 336 [1]–[37]
A range of paragraphs
Pinpointing by Page (no paragraph numbers) R v Leeds County Court, ex p Morris [1990] QB 523 (QB) 530–31
Page range after the court abbreviation
Citing a Particular Judge Arscott v The Coal Authority [2004] EWCA Civ 892, [2005] Env LR 6 [27] (Laws LJ)
Judge's name in round brackets after the paragraph pinpoint
1
Paragraph pinpoints go in square brackets [42] and sit at the very end of the citation, after all report references.
2
Page pinpoints (where no paragraph numbers exist) follow the court abbreviation in round brackets.
3
Judge names go in round brackets after the paragraph or page pinpoint: (Laws LJ), (Lord Hope).
Chapter II · Page 3 of 3 · European Courts

The skeleton cites
Strasbourg and Luxembourg.

Among the authorities is an ECHR case and a Court of Justice ruling. Neither follows the domestic format. Ms Okafor has left a Post-it: "European courts have their own rules. Learn them."

European Court of Human Rights
ECHR Citations Omojudi v UK (2009) 51 EHRR 10
Application decided and reported in EHRR series

Osman v UK ECHR 1998-VIII 3124
Official Reports of Judgments and Decisions format

Balogh v Hungary App no 47940/99 (ECHR, 20 July 2004)
Unreported: application number, court, full date
Court of Justice (EU)
CJEU Citation Case C-176/03 Commission v Council [2005] ECR I-7879, paras 47–48
Case number, then party names, then ECR reference, then paragraphs

You correct the footnotes carefully. The clock on the wall reads 9.04 am. You are making progress.

Chapter III · Page 1 of 3 · Acts of Parliament

Parliament speaks.
You must cite it correctly.

Generating scene...
AI-generated · Palace of Westminster at dusk

Harrison, the junior tenant, slides a bright orange statute book across your desk. "Legislation," he says, "is simpler than cases. But people still get it wrong. Watch."

Harrison, Junior Tenant Acts are cited by their short title and year. No italics, no quotation marks, no brackets around the year. The year is simply part of the name. Sections are abbreviated to a lower-case s, no full stop after.
Primary Legislation Act of Supremacy 1558
Title + year only. No brackets. No 'the' at the start.

Human Rights Act 1998, s 15(1)(b)
Section: lower-case 's', no full stop, subsections in round brackets

Equality Act 2010, ss 4–12
Multiple sections: 'ss', then the range
s
Sections
Use s for one section, ss for more than one. Never write sec or sect or §. Never add a full stop.
sch
Schedules
Abbreviate to sch. For a specific paragraph within a schedule: sch 2, para 4. No full stop after either abbreviation.
art
Articles
Used for EU instruments and some older legislation: art 5. No full stop; lower-case throughout.
Chapter III · Page 2 of 3 · Statutory Instruments and EU Legislation

Secondary legislation
and Brussels.

Harrison flicks to a later page. "Statutory instruments are cited like Acts, but you must add the SI number. And EU legislation? It has its own format entirely."

Statutory Instruments
SI Format: Title, SI Year/Number Penalties for Disorderly Behaviour (Amendment of Minimum Age) Order 2004, SI 2004/3166
Long title given in full. SI number: year/sequential number. No abbreviation of the title.
EU Primary and Secondary Legislation
EU Treaty and Regulations Consolidated Version of the Treaty on European Union [2008] OJ C115/13
Treaty title, then OJ reference: year, series letter, issue/first page

Council Regulation (EC) 139/2004 on the control of concentrations between undertakings (EC Merger Regulation) [2004] OJ L24/1, art 5
Full regulation title and description, OJ reference, then article pinpoint
1
Never abbreviate the long title of an Act or SI in OSCOLA, however unwieldy. Write it out in full every time.
2
OJ references follow the format: [year] OJ SeriesLetter Issue/FirstPage. The L series contains legislation; the C series contains communications and notices.
Chapter III · Page 3 of 3 · Command Papers and Law Commission Reports

The government
also publishes.

The skeleton cites a White Paper and a Law Commission Report. Harrison raises an eyebrow. "People always forget the series abbreviation and the number. Don't."

Command Papers Department for International Development, Eliminating World Poverty: Building our Common Future (White Paper, Cm 7656, 2009) ch 5
Author, title in italics, type + Cm number + year in round brackets, then pinpoint
Law Commission Reports Law Commission, Reforming Bribery (Law Com No 313, 2008) paras 3.12–3.17
Author, title in italics, report number + year in round brackets, paragraph pinpoint
"The format is always the same: author, title, publication details in brackets, then your pinpoint. Master this and you can cite almost anything official."

You ink through the errors on page seventeen. Harrison nods approvingly. "Good. Now for the hard part. Books and journals."

Chapter IV · Page 1 of 3 · Books

The library at
Lincoln's Inn.

Generating scene...
AI-generated · Barrister's study, candlelit law books

Ms Okafor summons you to the library. Three of your book citations are wrong. She spreads them on the table and circles the errors with a gold pen.

Ms Okafor QC A book citation has four elements in strict order: author, title in italics, publication details in round brackets, then pinpoint page. The author's name goes in the same form as in the publication itself. Not surname first, not initials only unless that is how the book prints it.
Standard Book Citation Thomas Hobbes, Leviathan (first published 1651, Penguin 1985) 268
Author as published, title italic, brackets: edition history + publisher + year, pinpoint page

Gareth Jones, Goff and Jones: The Law of Restitution (1st supp, 7th edn, Sweet & Maxwell 2009)
Supplement information and edition both go before the publisher

K Zweigert and H Kötz, An Introduction to Comparative Law (Tony Weir tr, 3rd edn, OUP 1998)
Translator before edition: 'tr' not 'trans.'

Hover over each element in the citation below to see what it represents.

HOVER EACH PART

K Zweigert and H KötzAuthors: initials then surname, exactly as printed. No full stops after initials. An Introduction to Comparative LawBook title: in italics. No quotation marks. (Tony Weir tr,Translator: forename surname then 'tr'. Goes before edition information. 3rd edn,Edition: abbreviated 'edn', no full stop. Goes after translator, before publisher. OUP 1998)Publisher then year, no comma between them. Close the round bracket here. 247Pinpoint page: just the number. No 'p.' or 'pp.' before it.
Chapter IV · Page 2 of 3 · Edited Collections and Encyclopedias

Not every chapter
belongs to the editor.

Ms Okafor slides two more volumes across the table. An edited collection. A volume of Halsbury's. "These need their own formats," she says, and waits.

Contribution to an Edited Collection Francis Rose, 'The Evolution of the Species' in Andrew Burrows and Alan Rodger (eds), Mapping the Law: Essays in Memory of Peter Birks (OUP 2006)
Chapter author, chapter title in single quotes, 'in' then editor(s) + '(eds)',
book title in italics, publication details in round brackets
1
The chapter title goes in single quotation marks, in Roman type (not italics).
2
The editors are introduced with 'in' and followed by (eds) for multiple editors or (ed) for one.
3
The book title is italicised; the chapter title is not. The distinction is deliberate and meaningful.
Encyclopedias
Halsbury's Laws Halsbury's Laws (5th edn, 2010) vol 57, para 53
Title italic, edition + year in brackets, then volume and paragraph pinpoint
Chapter IV · Page 3 of 3 · Journal Articles and Subsequent References

The article title
is not italicised.

The last group of errors are the journal articles. You have italicised the article titles. Ms Okafor shakes her head slowly. "Article titles go in single quotation marks. The journal title is italicised. The opposite of what you have done."

Standard Journal Article Paul Craig, 'Theory, "Pure Theory" and Values in Public Law' [2005] PL 440
Author, 'article title' in single quotes, [year] or (year), volume, Journal Title, first page

JAG Griffith, 'The Common Law and the Political Constitution' (2001) 117 LQR 42, 64
Pinpoint: comma between first page (42) and pinpoint page (64)

HOVER EACH PART

JAG GriffithAuthor initials then surname. No full stops after initials. 'The Common Law and the Political Constitution'Article title in single quotation marks, Roman type. Never italicised. (2001)Year in round brackets when volume number alone identifies the volume 117Volume number. No 'vol' prefix. No full stop. LQRJournal title in italics. Usually abbreviated (Law Quarterly Review = LQR). 42, 64First page of article (42), comma, then pinpoint page (64). No 'p.' prefix.
ibid and subsequent references
Repeating References ibid
Immediately preceding footnote, same page. Not italicised.

ibid 64
Immediately preceding footnote, different page.

Griffith (n 7) 64
Subsequent reference: Surname (n [footnote number]) pinpoint. No 'op cit'.
Chapter V · Page 1 of 3 · Websites and Online Sources

Not everything
is in a bound volume.

Generating scene...
AI-generated · Law library meets digital blue light

The final section of the skeleton argument cites a blog post, a government report only available online, and an online journal article. Ms Okafor has left three red question marks in the margin. "Online sources," she wrote underneath, "are cited only when no print version exists. And when you do cite them, the format must be exact."

Ms Okafor QC The URL goes in angle brackets. Not round brackets, not square brackets. Angle brackets. And the access date must appear at the end, in the format: accessed DD Month YYYY. If you forget the access date, the citation is worthless.
Website or Blog Post Sarah Cole, 'Virtual Friend Fires Employee' (Naked Law, 1 May 2009) <www.nakedlaw.com/2009/05/index.html> accessed 19 November 2009
Author, 'title', (site name, date), <URL>, accessed DD Month YYYY
< >
Angle Brackets
The URL always goes inside angle brackets <URL>. Do not hyperlink or underline it. Write it exactly as it appears.
📅
Access Date
Always write accessed DD Month YYYY at the very end. Day, full month name, four-digit year. No commas.
🖨
Print First
Cite online versions only when no print version exists. If a print version exists, cite that instead and omit the URL entirely.
Chapter V · Page 2 of 3 · Online Journals and Newspaper Articles

The journal is online.
The format adapts.

The online journal article needs a slightly different treatment from the blog post. And the newspaper article has its own rules entirely.

Online Journal Article Graham Greenleaf, 'The Global Development of Free Access to Legal Information' (2010) 1(1) EJLT <http://ejlt.org//article/view/17> accessed 27 July 2010
Author, 'article title', (year) volume(issue) Journal Title, <URL>, access date
No first-page number when the article exists only online
Newspaper Article Jane Croft, 'Supreme Court Warns on Quality' Financial Times (London, 1 July 2010) 3
Author, 'article title', Newspaper (Place, Date), page number
Newspaper title in italics; article title in single quotes, Roman type
1
Online journals that have no print counterpart: omit the first page number; the URL suffices to locate the article.
2
Newspapers: the place of publication goes in round brackets with the date, separated by a comma: (London, 1 July 2010). The page number follows outside the brackets.
3
Newspaper titles are italicised; article titles are in single quotation marks in Roman type. The same distinction applies as for journal articles.
Chapter V · Page 3 of 3 · The Brief is Ready

9.58 am.
You set down your pen.

The corrected skeleton argument sits in a neat stack on your desk. Forty pages. Every footnote checked. Every abbreviation without a full stop. Every pinpoint in its proper place. Every URL inside its angle brackets with an access date beside it.

Ms Okafor takes the document without a word and reads the first page. Then the second. Then she looks up.

Ms Okafor QC You understand OSCOLA. Cases. Statutes. Secondary legislation. EU material. Books. Edited collections. Journal articles. Online sources. Newspapers. The five chapters of citation. Now prove it. There is one final test.

She places a single printed sheet on your desk. Ten questions. A quiet room. The clock on the wall marks exactly ten o'clock.

This is your bar examination, Eleanor.

The Bar Examination · OSCOLA Mastery Assessment

Prove your
command of OSCOLA.

Generating scene...
AI-generated · Empty courtroom, morning light

Ten questions drawn from all five chapters. Select the correct answer. Feedback is given immediately after each choice.
